texts.orig: With the increasing concerns on global climate change, the control of anthropogenic emissions of greenhouse gases, particularly CO 2 , has received significant attention in both academia and industry in recent years. CO 2 emissions have increased dramatically since the industrial revolution and reach over 400 ppm in the atmosphere nowadays, accounting for more than a 30 % increase compared to that in 1958 ( Wang and Song, 2020 ). Fortunately, many countries submitted new intended emission reduction goals at the 21 st Conference of Parties in Paris, December 2015. To meet the 1.5 °C scenarios recommended by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) ( Masson-Delmotte et al., 2018 ), significant effort should be placed on the reduction of CO 2 emissions into the atmosphere. Carbon capture, utilization, and storage (CCUS) will be one of the most promising ways to mitigate CO 2 emissions with the vital advantage of continuously using fossil fuels without causing significantly increased CO 2 emissions ( He, 2018 ). The main application of CO 2 capture can be at large-scale CO 2 -emission sources: fossil fuel power plants and industries, especially energy-intensive processes such as steel/steel manufacturing and cement production( He et al., 2013 ). texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane systems for CO 2 capture have become increasingly attractive due to their higher energy efficiency, smaller footprint, and lower environmental impact. However, membrane systems should be operated at the optimal condition for a given separation scenario to achieve a significant cost-reduction benefit. This work paid particular attention to the insight of membrane system design and comparison of different membranes for various post-combustion CO 2 capture scenarios. The four membranes demonstrated at pilot scale have been investigated for carbon capture in the cement factory and fossil fuel-fired power plants, and the specific cost of < 20 $/tonne CO 2 captured for both Polaris and PolyActive membranes were found in the scenarios where the flue gases contain high-content CO 2 of > 13 vol.%. The membrane units with an estimated footprint of fewer than 7 standard 40-foot containers can be realistically installed in a cement plant. It is recommended that the deployment of membrane systems for CO 2 capture should be prioritized in the energy-intensive industries due to a relatively smaller gas volume to be processed compared to power plants. Moreover, membrane systems are better to be operated at a moderate CO 2 capture ratio of 70-80 % and pursuing a very high capture ratio of 90 % will not be economically beneficial.

Different technologies such as chemical absorption (e.g., monoethanolamine (MEA), methyldiethanolamine (MDEA), ionic liquids) ( Hospital-Benito et al., 2020 ; Idem et al., 2006 ; Zhao et al., 2016 ), physical adsorption (e.g., metal-organic frameworks, potassium-based adsorbents) ( Guo et al., 2021 ; Samanta et al., 2012 ), cryogenic distillation, and membrane separation have the potential to be used for CO 2 capture ( Baker et al., 2018 ; He, 2018 ). The technology maturity and advance are different, as stated by Figueroa et al. ( Figueroa et al., 2008 ). Today, amine-based solvents have been implemented at largeand full-scale worldwide, and some large projects (e.g., Boundary Dam, ROAD, Petra Nova Carbon Capture project) achieve the CO 2 capture rate of more than 1 Mtonne/year. However, it still faces the challenges of high energy-demanding and solvent degradation, which may result in a significant incremental cost and a high environmental impact. Some emerging separation technologies based on the novel absorbents of ionic liquids (high CO 2 solubility) and solid sorbents of microporous materials such as zeolite, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), and metal oxides (chemical looping cycle) have been recently investigated for CO 2 capture. Those third-generation materials showed good potential and cost reduction benefits, but most are in the early research phase. For a more detailed and in-depth understanding of absorption and adsorption materials for CO 2 capture, the readers can refer to the previous reviews ( Brunetti et al., 2010 ; Lai et al., 2021 ; Lee and Park, 2015 ; Wang et al., 2011 ). texts.orig: Choosing a suitable membrane material is mainly dependent on the feed gas compositions, process conditions, and specific separation requirements. If the high purity of products is required, a membrane with high selectivity will be preferred. While for the scenario with large quantities of gas to be treated, highly CO 2 -permeable membranes will be favorable to reduce the required membrane area, and thus reduce the membrane capital cost. CO 2 capture from flue gas in coal-fired power plants needs to be firstly tackled, while the rapid growth in gas-fired power generation indicates that carbon capture technologies should be expanded to the natural gas-fired power plants. However, those technologies are currently too expensive for the capital and operating cost, compared to the limited revenue-generating applications for CO 2 that are currently available (mainly enhanced oil or gas recovery (EOR/EGR)). The development of novel technologies with a lower cost of the membrane system is urgently needed to achieve full-scale deployment for carbon capture. Moreover, the cement industry represents 5 to 7 % of global anthropogenic CO 2 emissions. The applications of CO 2 capture in cement kilns could potentially result in a doubling of the price of cement in the market. Therefore, there is interest in the development of more cost-effective CO 2 capture solutions. In general, membranes with high CO 2 permeance and moderate selectivity are needed to design an economically viable process for producing high purity CO 2 (usually more than 95 vol.%) for storage or enhanced gas/oil recovery. Only a few membranes have been successfully demonstrated at the pilot scale for post-combustion carbon capture ( Dai et al., 2019 ; He, 2018 ; Janakiram et al., 2021 ; Scholes, 2020 ). Among them, MTR (Membrane Technology & Research, Inc.) built up a pilot membrane system for post-combustion carbon capture in a 1 MW coal-fired power plant and an NGCC power plant using their high permeable Polaris membranes ( Merkel et al., 2010 ). They demonstrated a less energy consumption and a lower cost process for capturing 80 % CO 2 from the NGCC power plant compared with typical amine processes. At Brevik, Norway, a pilot hollow fiber fixed-site-carrier (FSC) membrane system (membrane area: 20 m 2 ) has been tested at the Norcem cement plant where the CO 2 feed concentration is ca. 17 mol.% (wet-base) ( He, 2021 ). The system presented stable performance over the longterm testing of 6 months ( Hägg et al., 2017 ). Moreover, the Ohio State University (OSU) reported a 1.4 m 2 spiral-wound module fabricated from an amine-containing facilitated transport membrane tested with actual flue gas at the National Carbon Capture Center in Wilsonville ( Han et al., 2019a ). The PolyActive TM membranes (made from Poly (ethylene oxide)) from Helmholtz-Zentrum Geesthacht were also tested for CO 2 capture from real flue gas with a 12.5 m 2 membrane module ( Brinkmann et al., 2017 ; Pohlmann et al., 2016 ). Those efforts significantly promote bringing gas separation membrane technology into future commercial CO 2 capture applications. However, membrane system performance depends not only on the membrane material properties but also on the process configurations and operating conditions. Therefore, before starting with the full-scale deployment of membrane systems for post-combustion CO 2 capture, it is crucial to conduct process design and simulation to document the techno-economic feasibility at a full-scale plant based on the benchor pilot-scale experimental data and identify the optimal operating condition for a specific scenario. Some literature has already reported the process design and feasibility analysis on membrane systems for CO 2 capture from flue gas. He et al. reported the two-stage membrane system for CO 2 capture from flue gases based on HYSYS integrated with their in-house membrane model of ChemBrane and their simulation results indicated a promising potential for the economic feasibility compared to the amine absorption ( He et al., 2015 ; He and Hägg, 2011 ). Merkel et al. reported a three-stage membrane system with air sweep to achieve high CO 2 recovery and purity while being economically competitive with existing commercial carbon cap- ture technology ( Merkel et al., 2010 ). However, the designed process needs to retrofit existing power plants by integrating high CO 2 content air into the boiler, which increases the process operation complexity. Thus, techno-economic feasibility for such a complex process should be further investigated. A two-stage membrane system with the integration of compression heat showed a nice potential for CO 2 capture from a refinery plant with 18260 kmol/h flue gas at a specific cost of 48.7 $/ tonne CO 2 ( He et al., 2015 ). Moreover, the techno-economic feasibility analysis of membrane-based processes for CO 2 capture has been reported by Asadi and Kazempoor ( Asadi and Kazempoor, 2021 ), and the most cost-effective design with a CO 2 capture cost of 22.76 $/tonne CO 2 was determined. However, the process design, simulation basis (e.g., membrane performance, operating condition, and feed composition, etc.) cost model, and separation requirement (such as purity and capture ratio) reported in those work were different, which makes it difficult to judge which membrane may have great potential for a specific application. Therefore, in this work, a systematic comparison of four types of membranes (demonstrated at high technology readiness levels (TRL) of 5-7) was conducted to evaluate their technology advance for full-scale CO 2 capture applications. Considering the difference in the material performance, CO 2 capture the flue gases come from three processes (i.e., coal-fired power plant, natural gas-fired plant, and cement factory) using membrane systems were designed, and the influences of feed composition and operating parameters were investigated by process simulation. In the end, the best membrane processes were proposed based on the cost estimation for different CO 2 capture from different flue gases.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane systems for CO 2 capture are usually located in a 'tail-end ' downstream process after the necessary pre-treatment such as electrostatic precipitator (ESP), DeNO x, and flue gas desulphurization (FGD) systems. Even though membrane systems offer a potentially lower cost and more energy-efficient option for CO 2 capture, there are still challenges related to the limited applications of membrane systems in postcombustion CO 2 capture as shown in Table 1 . Thus, the selection of suitable membrane materials will be dependent on the flue gas feed composition (especially CO 2 , O 2 , water vapor, SO x , NO x , etc.) and operating parameters (e.g., temperature, pressure). The flue gases from coalfired power plants usually have low pressure (just above the atmospheric pressure) and contain ca. 10-14 vol.% CO 2 . Thus, the driving force for the CO 2 transport through a solution-diffusion (S-D) membrane is low if no feed compression and/or vacuum suction on the permeate is applied. Even though the facilitated transport (FT) membranes present high gas permeance due to the extra FT contribution, the overall permeate flux is still low due to a lower net driving force when the system is operated at a low feed pressure. In addition, the chemical stability by exposure to the impurities such as SO 2 , NO x which usually exist in flue gases is also challenging related to the membrane durability and lifetime. Therefore, highly CO 2 -permeable, highly CO 2 -selective, and chemically stable membranes are required for membrane systems to be competitive with other CO 2 capture technologies such as the benchmark amine absorption. It is worth noting that the combustion of natural gas produces flue gas with a much lower CO 2 content (e.g., < 5 vol.% ( Scholes et al., 2016 )), which results in a very low feed CO 2 partial pressure if no significant feed compression is employed. Fortunately, this type of flue gas is relatively clean as there is no/little SO X , NO x , fly ash, and metal traces. Thus, the membrane lifetime might be longer in such applications. The calcination process for the cement production releases CO 2 directly from the heating of limestone, which accounts for ∼ 50 % of all CO 2 emissions; while burning fossil fuels (coal and biomass) to heat the kiln indirectly contributes to another part of CO 2 emissions. texts.orig: Based on the flue gas characteristics listed in Table 1 , the integration of membrane systems with the proper pre-treatment units is designed for post-combustion CO 2 capture as shown in Fig. 1 a. The flue gas after pretreatment (e.g., deNOx, FGD) is cooled down (cooler) to remove part of water and pass through a filter before sending it into the compressors, which prevents liquid water from entering the compressor. However, the induced pressure drop in the filters is 'choking ' the compressor on its suction side (the inlet pressure of compressors usually should be higher than 850 mbar) and care must be taken in the real design of a potential full-scale carbon capture process. If the flue gas is quite dirty (e.g., in a cement factory), a high-temperature pre-filter should be installed in front of the cooler to avoid the risk of severe fouling/scaling. Again, the pressure drop on the gas side of the cooler should be also considered. Due to the large cost difference between a standard air compressor and an acid gas (especially SO x , NO x , and CO 2 in the condensed water) approved compressor, avoiding the water condensation inside compres- sors is crucial if the carbon-steel centrifugal compressors are designed for real-life applications. For the facilitated transport membranes, humidification might be applied before feeding it into the membrane unit to maintain a high water-content feed gas. A heat exchanger might be installed to adapt the humidified flue gas to the membrane operating temperature as shown in Fig. 1 a. The CO 2 -lean retentate stream can be sent to a turboexpander for recovering work or vented directly. On the membrane permeate side, a vacuum pump or a sweep gas can be applied to generate a sufficiently high trans-membrane pressure ratio (i.e., the ratio between feed and permeate pressure). It should be noted that vacuum pumps are preferred to avoid the re-mixing of the capture CO 2 with any sweep gas. Condensers are usually equipped after the vacuum pump to collect the condensed water as the dew point is crossed and a two-phase flow is encountered during the isothermal recompression to 1 bar in the outlet of a vacuum pump. The enriched CO 2 stream will be re-compressed and sent to other separation units (e.g., cryogenics) or a second-stage membrane unit for CO 2 polishing to meet the purity required for the downstream CO 2 utilization or transport and storage as shown in Fig. 1 b & 1 c. In the case of the twoor multi-stage membrane systems, the inter-stage re-humidification should be designed to maintain a high separation performance for the FT membranes where water vapor is acting as a catalyst to provide a significant contribution for the CO 2 permeation based on the facilitated transport
texts.text: Based on the flue gas characteristics listed in Table 1 , the integration of membrane systems with the proper pre-treatment units is designed for post-combustion CO 2 capture as shown in Fig. 1 a. The flue gas after pretreatment (e.g., deNOx, FGD) is cooled down (cooler) to remove part of water and pass through a filter before sending it into the compressors, which prevents liquid water from entering the compressor. However, the induced pressure drop in the filters is 'choking ' the compressor on its suction side (the inlet pressure of compressors usually should be higher than 850 mbar) and care must be taken in the real design of a potential full-scale carbon capture process. If the flue gas is quite dirty (e.g., in a cement factory), a high-temperature pre-filter should be installed in front of the cooler to avoid the risk of severe fouling/scaling. Again, the pressure drop on the gas side of the cooler should be also considered. Due to the large cost difference between a standard air compressor and an acid gas (especially SO x , NO x , and CO 2 in the condensed water) approved compressor, avoiding the water condensation inside compres- sors is crucial if the carbon-steel centrifugal compressors are designed for real-life applications. For the facilitated transport membranes, humidification might be applied before feeding it into the membrane unit to maintain a high water-content feed gas. A heat exchanger might be installed to adapt the humidified flue gas to the membrane operating temperature as shown in Fig. 1 a. The CO 2 -lean retentate stream can be sent to a turboexpander for recovering work or vented directly. On the membrane permeate side, a vacuum pump or a sweep gas can be applied to generate a sufficiently high trans-membrane pressure ratio (i.e., the ratio between feed and permeate pressure). It should be noted that vacuum pumps are preferred to avoid the re-mixing of the capture CO 2 with any sweep gas. Condensers are usually equipped after the vacuum pump to collect the condensed water as the dew point is crossed and a two-phase flow is encountered during the isothermal recompression to 1 bar in the outlet of a vacuum pump. The enriched CO 2 stream will be re-compressed and sent to other separation units (e.g., cryogenics) or a second-stage membrane unit for CO 2 polishing to meet the purity required for the downstream CO 2 utilization or transport and storage as shown in Fig. 1 b & 1 c. In the case of the twoor multi-stage membrane systems, the inter-stage re-humidification should be designed to maintain a high separation performance for the FT membranes where water vapor is acting as a catalyst to provide a significant contribution for the CO 2 permeation based on the facilitated transport

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2. Simulation basis
texts.text: 2.2. Simulation basis

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Three different scenarios: an advanced supercritical (ASC) coal-fired power plant with a gross output of 819 Mwe without CO 2 capture reported in the CESAR project ( Manzolini et al., 2015 ), natural gas combined-cycle (NGCC) power plant ( Scholes et al., 2016 ), and cement factory ( Udara et al., 2020 ) were chosen for process simulations, and the characteristics of the flue gases are shown in Table 2 . Only the main components of CO 2 , N 2 , O 2, and water vapor are considered to simplify process simulation due to the lack of permeation data for SO x and NO x . Membrane materials can be tailored or functionalized to meet the separation requirement for CO 2 capture from flue gas, and different membranes such as nanocomposite membranes, FT membranes, mixed matrix membranes, and carbon membranes have been intensively investigated in the last decade for CO 2 separations ( He, 2018 ). Even though many pieces of literature reported high-performance membranes from the lab-scale tests that exceed the Robeson upper bound for the CO 2 /N 2 pair ( Han and Ho, 2021 ), they are not necessarily the best membranes for industrial applications. Therefore, only four types of membranes that have been tested at the pilot scale in real-life industries were chosen as the simulation basis listed in Table 2 . For the Polaris and PolyActive membrane systems, water vapor should be removed from flue gas before feeding it into the membrane unit, while for the FSC and FT-PVAm membranes, high water vapor contents (i.e., water-saturated or a high relative humidity of > 90 % at the simulated temperature and pressures) should be maintained in the feed gas stream to get high CO 2 permeance that is significantly attributed to the FT mechanism. Water vapor permeance has been seldom reported in the literature, and thus a 10X of CO 2 permeance ( Brinkmann et al., 2015 ; He, 2021 ) was assumed in this work. Moreover, a CO 2 /N 2 selectivity of 10 was applied for the Polaris and FT-PVAm membranes that have no experimental data been reported. It should be noted that those separation performances are dependent on the membrane testing temperature, therefore, the specific temperature for each type of membrane was applied even though the cooling or heating energy has not been estimated in the simulations. Moreover, the gas permeances (especially for CO 2 ) may also be influenced by feed pressure and concentration, as well as the permeate operation mode (i.e., using sweep gas or vacuum pump)-this is, however, not considered in the current work to simplify the process simulations.

texts.orig: where D A and D AC are the diffusion coefficients of Fickian diffusion and carrier-mediated (complex) diffusion, respectively, and l is the membrane thickness of a selective layer. For the FSC and FT-PVAm membranes, the carrier (-NH 2 ) is chemically bonded to the polymer matrix. A schematic diagram for the CO 2 separation from through an FT membrane is shown in Fig. 2 b, where CO 2 passes through a membrane that is based on the combination of FT and S-D mechanisms when the water molecules are presenting, while the non-reactive gases such as N 2 and O 2 can only transport via the solution-diffusion mechanism. Thus, this type of membrane shows both high CO 2 permeance and high selectivities of CO 2 over the other gas molecules. However, the carrier saturation phenomenon directs to the application of a moderate feed pressure (e.g., < 3 bar) for the FT membranes to avoid a very low absolute water content inside the membrane matrix ( He and Hägg, 2014 ). It is also expected that the diffusion coefficient will be significantly enhanced by the increase of temperature, while the selectivity may be reduced due to a decreased CO 2 solubility. Therefore, the operating temperature needs to be investigated to identify the best operating condition. It should be noted that the applied membrane model does not consider the facilitated transport item, therefore, the separation process is simplified by a conventional S-D mechanism without the effect from the carrier-mediation contribution as the apparent gas permeances have been reported from gas permeation tests ( Asadi and Kazempoor, 2021 ). Moreover, a constant membrane performance (permeance and selectivity) at a given temperature was simulated due to the lack of the pressureand concentration-dependent experimental data-this is acceptable as the simulation window for these parameters in the current work is relatively narrow. The following assumptions are made to simplify the simulations.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Capital cost is estimated based on the major equipment for the membrane system (e.g., compressor, vacuum pump, and membrane unit), which is used for the preliminary economic feasibility analysis. A schematic illustration of cost estimation for CO 2 capture with membrane system is shown in Fig. 3 . The membrane system performance is influenced by the material properties of selectivity ( S ) and gas permeance ( Pe ), the operating parameters (temperature ( T ), pressure( p )), and the separation scenarios (feed flow and composition). Based on the process simulation, the required membrane area and power demands can be obtained to complete the given separation requirement (i.e., CO 2 purity and capture ratio). Based on the output variables illustrated in Fig. 3 , a suitable cost model was then applied to estimate the specific CO 2 capture cost.

It should be noted that the required membrane area and power demand for the cement plant scenario are much smaller due to a smaller gas volume to be processed as shown in Fig. 4 a and 4 b. The slight difference in the power demand comes from the variation of the membrane operating temperatures of 20-67 °C. The total gas volume feeding into the membrane unit is slightly different due to the difference in the water content (The condensers of V100 and V103 are used to remove the condensed water from flue gas (see Fig. S1)). For the membrane unit footprint, except for the FT-PVAm membrane, the other three membranes present a very similar footprint as shown in Fig. 4 d. Even though the required FSC membrane area is the largest, the higher packing density (estimated 2000 m 2 /m 3 ) for the hollow fiber modules still makes the system relatively compact. Moreover, both FT-PVAm and FSC membranes show a higher CO 2 purity due to a much higher CO 2 /N 2 selectivity compared to the Polaris and PolyActive membranes as shown in Fig. 4 c. It is worth noting that the enriched CO 2 can only reach the purity of < 40 vol.% on the permeate side for the NGCC plant scenario with a much lower feed CO 2 content, which makes it difficult to get high purity CO 2 followed by the second stage membrane unit (unless a multi-stage membrane system is applied). It was reported that the CO 2 purity should reach ca. 60 vol.% in the first stage to achieve the separation requirement of 95 vol.% CO 2 in the final product ( He et al., 2015 ). Therefore, the two-stage membrane systems might not be technically feasible to complete the CO 2 capture in the NGCC plant and thus were not further investigated. While the simulations of the two-stage membrane systems for CO 2 capture in both ASC power plant and cement plant were conducted to reach the high purity CO 2 of 95 vol.% at different CO 2 capture ratios.

Therefore, the two-stage membrane systems might not be technically feasible to complete the CO 2 capture in the NGCC plant and thus were not further investigated. While the simulations of the two-stage membrane systems for CO 2 capture in both ASC power plant and cement plant were conducted to reach the high purity CO 2 of 95 vol.% at different CO 2 capture ratios.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2. Techno-economic feasibility analysis
texts.text: 3.2. Techno-economic feasibility analysis

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Two scenarios (i.e., ASC power plant and cement plant) have been investigated with these four membranes for CO 2 capture, and the designed two-stage membrane system (see Fig. S2) was simulated to achieve the separation requirement with a capture ratio of 70 % and a CO 2 purity of 95 vol.%. The five key parameters (i.e., membrane area, specific energy consumption, footprint, O 2 content in the CO 2 product, and specific cost) were obtained based on the UniSim simulations together with the cost estimation, and the results are shown in Figs. 5 and 6 (The relative factor is defined as the ratio between the estimated results and the corresponding reference values, and the original data can be referred to Table S5 and S6 in the Supporting Information). It can be found that the specific CO 2 capture cost for the Polaris and PolyActive membranes is lower than 20 $/tonne CO 2 captured for both scenarios, which is slightly more cost-effective compared to the FSC and FT-PVAm membranes. The membrane unit footprint in the cement plant scenario of < 7 containers (Size: standard 40-foot) is expected to be much smaller compared to a conventional amine absorber, and the specific energy consumption of ca. 0.7 GJ/tonne CO 2 captured indicates the high energy efficiency (see Fig. 5 ). It is clear that both specific cost and energy consumption are higher for the ASC power plant scenario due to a lower CO 2 content in the feed flue gas. Therefore, industrial decarbonization might be prioritized to be deployed in the cement factory, and further moved towards coal-fired power plants with significant CO 2 emissions.
texts.text: Two scenarios (i.e., ASC power plant and cement plant) have been investigated with these four membranes for CO 2 capture, and the designed two-stage membrane system (see Fig. S2) was simulated to achieve the separation requirement with a capture ratio of 70 % and a CO 2 purity of 95 vol.%. texts.label: text
texts.orig: It should be noted that the impurity of O 2 is quite high (0.58-3.4 vol.%) in the final CO 2 product, which is extremely challenging for the downstream CO 2 utilization (e.g., CO 2 hydrogenation to methanol) or the enhanced oil or gas recovery (it usually requires a very low O 2 content of < 50 ppm). Most of the previous research focused on developing highly permeable membranes with a high CO 2 /N 2 selectivity, while little effort has been put into enhancing the CO 2 /O 2 selectivity. Moreover, all four membranes were demonstrated at a pilot scale using single-stage membrane systems without the implementation of CO 2 transport and utilization, in which the CO 2 purity on the permeate is ca. 60-70 vol.%. Therefore, future work to bring the membrane technology to higher TRL using twoor multi-stage membrane systems for CO 2 capture should be conducted to prove its techno-economic feasibility. Moreover, the results also indicate that the design of hybrid systems such as membrane-absorption ( Freeman et al., 2014 ), membranecryogenics ( Scholes et al., 2013 ; Song et al., 2021 ) might be necessary to reach the separation requirement with a very low O 2 content in the captured CO 2 stream. For such cases, developing novel membrane materials with higher CO 2 permeances will be more crucial than pursuing a high CO 2 /N 2 selectivity, and the CO 2 enrichment with the membrane bulk separation may not be necessary to achieve 60 vol.% as the high CO 2 content feed gas could significantly reduce the amine separation performance ( Nuchitprasittichai and Cremaschi, 2013 )-this has not been discussed in the current work.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.3. Sensitivity of CO 2 capture ratio
texts.text: 3.3. Sensitivity of CO 2 capture ratio

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Based on the above techno-economic feasibility analysis, it can be concluded that all four membranes have the potential to be commercialized for CO 2 capture at a moderate CO 2 capture ratio of 70 % in the cement plants with an acceptable footprint for the membrane units. To identify the optimal capture ratio for different membrane systems, the sensitivity analysis by varying the CO 2 capture ratio from 50-90 % was conducted, and the simulation results are shown in Fig. 7 . The specific energy consumption decreases with the increase of CO 2 capture ratio up to 80 % for all the membrane systems as shown in Fig. 7 a, and the optimal CO 2 capture ratio in the range of 70-80 % was identified based on the results in Fig. 7 b. The benefit from the increased CO 2 capture flow rate cannot offset the dramatically increased membrane area, especially when OPEX (related to electricity cost) has no significant contribution to the cost reduction at a higher CO 2 capture ratio of > 80 %. Therefore, a moderate CO 2 capture ratio at 70-80 % is recommended for the designed membrane systems.
texts.text: Based on the above techno-economic feasibility analysis, it can be concluded that all four membranes have the potential to be commercialized for CO 2 capture at a moderate CO 2 capture ratio of 70 % in the cement plants with an acceptable footprint for the membrane units. To identify the optimal capture ratio for different membrane systems, the sensitivity analysis by varying the CO 2 capture ratio from 50-90 % was conducted, and the simulation results are shown in Fig. 7 . The specific energy consumption decreases with the increase of CO 2 capture ratio up to 80 % for all the membrane systems as shown in Fig. 7 a, and the optimal CO 2 capture ratio in the range of 70-80 % was identified based on the results in Fig. 7 b. The benefit from the increased CO 2 capture flow rate cannot offset the dramatically increased membrane area, especially when OPEX (related to electricity cost) has no significant contribution to the cost reduction at a higher CO 2 capture ratio of > 80 %. Therefore, a moderate CO 2 capture ratio at 70-80 % is recommended for the designed membrane systems.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.4. Permeate pressure influence
texts.text: 3.4. Permeate pressure influence

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The Polaris membrane system presents the lowest CO 2 capture cost in different scenarios and can be considered as the state-of-the-art membrane technology for post-combustion CO 2 capture. Even though the moderate feed pressure of 2 bar was set for the simulation to avoid a significantly high energy consumption for the flue gas compression, it is worth investigating the permeate pressure influence on the system performance and identifying the optimal pressure ratio for a specific application. Moreover, the membrane area for the second-stage unit was found much smaller than that of the first-stage unit (see Table S7), so the influence of the membrane cost from the second-stage unit is expected to be smaller when the driving force changes at different permeate pressures. Therefore, only the first stage permeate pressure varying from 10-30 kPa was investigated for the Polaris membrane to reach the CO 2 purity and capture ratio of 95 vol.% and 70 %, respectively. The simulation results in Fig. 8 a indicate that pursuing a very high vacuum may dramatically increase the energy consumption from the vacuum pumps (i.e., OPEX), which cannot be offset by the reduced membrane area (related to the membrane-based annual CAPEX). Thus, the overall trade-off gives the optimal permeate pressure of 20 kPa (as shown in Fig. 8 b)this operating condition can be achieved by the commercial vacuum pumps.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The four types of membranes (Polaris, PolyActive, FT-PVAm, and FSC) demonstrated at high TRL of 5-7 have been selected for the technoeconomic feasibility analysis in post-combustion CO 2 capture from fossil fuel-fired plants and cement factories. The single-stage membrane system was designed for the comparison of different membrane systems for the CO 2 enrichment, which can be further purified with a secondstage membrane unit or by other separation technologies. It was found that a single-stage membrane system can reach 60 vol.% CO 2 in the permeate stream when the flue gases containing higher CO 2 content in an ASC power plant and a cement plant. while it is challenging to reach 40 vol.% CO 2 for the scenario of the NGCC plant due to the extremely low feed CO 2 content of 5 %. It is also recommended that CO 2 capture with membrane systems should be prioritized to be implemented in the cement factory due to its smaller gas stream. The designed two-stage membrane systems were found to be technically feasible to reach the CO 2 purity of 95 vol.% at a moderate CO 2 capture ratio of 70 %. The specific CO 2 capture cost for the Polaris and PolyActive membranes is lower than 20 $/tonne CO 2 captured, which is slightly cost-effective compared with the FSC and FT-PVAm membranes. Moreover, the small membrane unit footprint of fewer than 7 containers (standard 40-foot size) is realistic to be installed in the plant. The simulation results also indicate that the membrane system is an energy-efficient process by considering a low specific energy consumption of ca. 0.7 GJ/tonne CO 2 captured. However, the impurity of O 2 in the final CO 2 product should not be neglected when it comes to downstream CO 2 utilization. Thus, developing membranes with high CO 2 /O 2 selectivity or designing hybrid membrane-cryogenic systems is urgently needed. Moreover, the membrane system should be operated at an optimal CO 2 capture ratio of 7080 % instead of pursuing a very high value of 90 %. To balance the CAPEX and OPEX, maintaining an appropriate pressure ratio of 10 by applying a permeate vacuum pressure of 20 kPa in the first stage membrane unit is preferable for commercial application.
texts.text: The four types of membranes (Polaris, PolyActive, FT-PVAm, and FSC) demonstrated at high TRL of 5-7 have been selected for the technoeconomic feasibility analysis in post-combustion CO 2 capture from fossil fuel-fired plants and cement factories. The single-stage membrane system was designed for the comparison of different membrane systems for the CO 2 enrichment, which can be further purified with a secondstage membrane unit or by other separation technologies. It was found that a single-stage membrane system can reach 60 vol.% CO 2 in the permeate stream when the flue gases containing higher CO 2 content in an ASC power plant and a cement plant. while it is challenging to reach 40 vol.% CO 2 for the scenario of the NGCC plant due to the extremely low feed CO 2 content of 5 %. It is also recommended that CO 2 capture with membrane systems should be prioritized to be implemented in the cement factory due to its smaller gas stream. The designed two-stage membrane systems were found to be technically feasible to reach the CO 2 purity of 95 vol.% at a moderate CO 2 capture ratio of 70 %. The specific CO 2 capture cost for the Polaris and PolyActive membranes is lower than 20 $/tonne CO 2 captured, which is slightly cost-effective compared with the FSC and FT-PVAm membranes. Moreover, the small membrane unit footprint of fewer than 7 containers (standard 40-foot size) is realistic to be installed in the plant. The simulation results also indicate that the membrane system is an energy-efficient process by considering a low specific energy consumption of ca. 0.7 GJ/tonne CO 2 captured. However, the impurity of O 2 in the final CO 2 product should not be neglected when it comes to downstream CO 2 utilization. Thus, developing membranes with high CO 2 /O 2 selectivity or designing hybrid membrane-cryogenic systems is urgently needed. Moreover, the membrane system should be operated at an optimal CO 2 capture ratio of 7080 % instead of pursuing a very high value of 90 %. To balance the CAPEX and OPEX, maintaining an appropriate pressure ratio of 10 by applying a permeate vacuum pressure of 20 kPa in the first stage membrane unit is preferable for commercial application.
